# fastactor

> Elixir/OTP-style actors for Python, built on [anyio](https://anyio.readthedocs.io/). Processes with mailboxes; `GenServer` (`call`/`cast`), `GenEvent` (multi-handler event manager), `GenStage` (demand-driven backpressured pipelines), `GenStateMachine` (`gen_statem`-style FSMs); supervisors with `one_for_one` / `one_for_all` / `rest_for_one`, `DynamicSupervisor`, `Registry` (unique / duplicate), `Agent`, awaitable `Task`, links, monitors, `trap_exits`, `handle_continue`, named registration, and optional OpenTelemetry tracing — all faithful to the OTP semantics you know from Elixir, translated to idiomatic Python with `async` / `await` and `anyio` task groups.

This file is **navigation, not a reference manual**. It gives you the mental model, where each piece lives, and the cross-cutting gotchas. For per-primitive APIs, read [`src/fastactor/otp/README.md`](src/fastactor/otp/README.md). For testing patterns, [`src/tests/README.md`](src/tests/README.md). For authoritative behavior, [`src/tests/otp/SPEC.md`](src/tests/otp/SPEC.md). To learn the model end-to-end, work through the 12-part walkthrough at [`src/tests/tutorial/`](src/tests/tutorial/README.md).

---

## Orientation

- **Top-level imports:** `from fastactor import run, settings, telemetry`. The actor surface is a separate package: `from fastactor.otp import ...`.
- **Runtime requirement:** every actor operation requires an active `Runtime` context (`async with Runtime(): ...`). Exactly one Runtime may be active per process.
- **Concurrency model:** `anyio` structured concurrency. Every actor runs inside the Runtime's `TaskGroup`; cancellation and teardown are propagated structurally.
- **Python:** 3.13+.
- **Mailbox:** bounded anyio memory object stream, capacity from `settings.mailbox_size` (default 1024, env var `FASTACTOR_MAILBOX_SIZE`).
- **Framework envelopes:** built-in protocol messages are tagged `msgspec.Struct`s carrying `sender_id: Pid | None` plus optional `metadata`. Use `message.sender_id` for stable identity; `message.sender` is a convenience that resolves back to the live local `Process` when possible. Raw `proc.send(obj)` still places arbitrary Python objects on the mailbox unwrapped.

### Where to go next

- **Learn the model:** [`src/tests/tutorial/`](src/tests/tutorial/README.md) — 12 lessons that are also runnable tests.
- **Per-primitive API:** [`src/fastactor/otp/README.md`](src/fastactor/otp/README.md) — contracts, return shapes, examples.
- **Authoritative behavior:** [`src/tests/otp/SPEC.md`](src/tests/otp/SPEC.md) — behaviour spec used to drive development.
- **Testing patterns:** [`src/tests/README.md`](src/tests/README.md) — fixtures, doubles, deterministic-sync idioms.

---

## Mental model — Elixir ↔ fastactor (core)

Both columns show the **call you actually write** — your subclass / module on the user-code rows, the framework name on the framework rows. Advanced primitives (`GenEvent`, `GenStage`, `GenStateMachine`) are summarized in their own section below.

| Elixir / OTP                                            | fastactor                                                 | Notes                                                                                        |
| ------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `MyWorker.start_link(arg)`                              | `await MyWorker.start_link(arg)`                          | Always `await`. `start_link` is inherited — you call it on your subclass.                    |
| `GenServer.call(pid, :req)`                             | `await worker.call(request, timeout=5)`                   | Raises on timeout or on callback exception.                                                  |
| `GenServer.cast(pid, :req)`                             | `worker.cast(request)`                                    | Fire-and-forget, synchronous call (not awaitable).                                           |
| `handle_call({:foo, bar}, _from, state)`                | `handle_call(self, call: Call)` — match `call.message`    | Bare return value becomes the reply; return `None` to defer (see below).                     |
| `{:noreply, state}` / `GenServer.reply(from, v)`        | `return None`, then later `call.set_result(v)`            | No sentinel class — `None` means "don't reply yet"; the server stashes the `Call` and resolves it later. Internally `Call` carries a `ref` and `set_result` resolves the caller's pending slot. |
| `{:stop, reason, reply, state}`                         | `return (reply, Stop(reason))`                            | Caller gets `reply`, then the server stops with `reason`. Symmetric with `(reply, Continue(term))`. |
| `{:noreply, state, {:continue, arg}}`                   | `return Continue("arg")`                                  | `handle_continue` runs next, before the next mailbox read.                                   |
| `{:via, Registry, {R, key}}`                            | `await Worker.start(via=("R", key))`                      | Mutually exclusive with `name=`. Also returns by `whereis(("R", key))`.                      |
| `Process.link(pid)` / `Process.monitor(pid)`            | `proc.link(other)` / `proc.monitor(other)`                | Bidirectional link; unidirectional monitor emits `Down` when the target terminates.          |
| `Process.flag(:trap_exit, true)`                        | `await MyWorker.start_link(trap_exits=True)`              | Convert link cascades to `handle_exit(message: Exit)` callbacks.                             |
| `Supervisor.start_link(..., strategy: :one_for_one)`    | `await Supervisor.start(strategy="one_for_one")`          | Plus `one_for_all` and `rest_for_one`.                                                       |
| `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(sup, spec)`              | `await dsup.start_child(spec)`                            | Always `one_for_one`; supports `max_children` and `extra_arguments`.                         |
| `Registry.start_link(keys: :unique, name: R)`           | `await Registry.new("R", mode="unique")`                  | Two modes: `unique` (1 pid per key) and `duplicate` (pub/sub fan-out).                       |
| `Agent.start_link(fn -> 0 end)`                         | `await Agent.start(lambda: 0)`                            | Tiny state-holder over `GenServer`.                                                          |
| `Task.async(fn)` / `Task.async_nolink(fn)`              | `await Task.start_link(fn)` / `await Task.start(fn)`      | `start_link` cascades a crash to caller; `start` does not. Consume with `result = await t`.  |

The names match where the Python idiom allows; divergences are intentional and annotated.

---

## Advanced primitives at a glance

These exist alongside `GenServer` for the cases where it isn't the right shape. Pick by intent:

- **`GenEvent`** — Erlang `gen_event`. One process hosts many `EventHandler` instances, each with its own state. Dispatch a single event to every handler in install order with `notify` (async) or `sync_notify` (await dispatch). Handler callbacks return `None` to keep, `RemoveHandler()` to drop, or `SwapHandler(args1, handler2, args2)` to atomically replace the handler. A handler that raises is removed; the manager keeps running. Source: [`gen_event.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_event.py). Public types: `GenEvent`, `EventHandler`, `RemoveHandler`, `SwapHandler`, `HandlerNotInstalled`.

- **`GenStage`** — Erlang `gen_stage`. Demand-driven, backpressured pipeline stages wired by subscription. Three roles share the `GenStage` base: `Producer[E]` (overrides `handle_demand`), `Consumer[E]` (calls `subscribe_to`, overrides `handle_events`), and `ProducerConsumer[In, Out]` (both). Routing is controlled by a dispatcher: `DemandDispatcher` (greedy, by outstanding demand), `BroadcastDispatcher` (every subscriber gets the same batch), or `PartitionDispatcher` (route by `hash_fn(event)`). `handle_demand` / `handle_events` may return a list or be `async def` generators yielding events. Wire envelopes: `Subscribe`, `SubscribeAck`, `Demand`, `Events`, `Cancel`. Source: [`gen_stage.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_stage.py).

- **`GenStateMachine`** — Erlang `gen_statem`. Finite state machines with two callback modes (set on the class via `callback_mode`): `"handle_event_function"` (one `handle_event(event_type, event_content, state, data)`) or `"state_functions"` (one method per state). Either mode optionally adds `"state_enter"` for enter-actions. Callbacks return action lists composed of: `Reply(to=call, reply=...)`, `NextEvent(type, content)`, `Postpone()`, `StateTimeout(time, content)`, `Timeout(time, content, name=...)`, `Hibernate()` (no-op in Python; for API parity). Source: [`gen_state_machine.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_state_machine.py).

These primitives don't yet have dedicated sections in `otp/README.md` — read the source docstrings and tests at [`src/tests/otp/test_gen_event.py`](src/tests/otp/test_gen_event.py), [`test_gen_stage.py`](src/tests/otp/test_gen_stage.py), [`test_gen_state_machine.py`](src/tests/otp/test_gen_state_machine.py).

---

## Primitive index

### Core primitives

| Primitive                     | What it does                                                         | Source                                                                                          | Docs                                                                                         |
| ----------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Process`                     | Base actor — mailbox, lifecycle, links, monitors                     | [`process.py`](src/fastactor/otp/process.py)                                                    | [README §Process](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#process)                                       |
| `GenServer`                   | `Process` + synchronous `call` + fire-and-forget `cast`              | [`gen_server.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_server.py)                                              | [README §GenServer](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#genserver)                                   |
| Links, monitors, `trap_exits` | OTP process relationships                                            | [`process.py`](src/fastactor/otp/process.py)                                                    | [README §Links, monitors, trap_exits](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#links-monitors-trap_exits) |
| `Supervisor`                  | OTP supervisor — 3 strategies, restart intensity                     | [`supervisor.py`](src/fastactor/otp/supervisor.py)                                              | [README §Supervisor](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#supervisor)                                 |
| `DynamicSupervisor`           | `one_for_one`-only, runtime-added children                           | [`dynamic_supervisor.py`](src/fastactor/otp/dynamic_supervisor.py)                              | [README §DynamicSupervisor](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#dynamicsupervisor)                   |
| `Registry`                    | Keyed name → process map, `unique` or `duplicate`                    | [`registry.py`](src/fastactor/otp/registry.py)                                                  | [README §Registry](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#registry)                                     |
| `Agent`                       | Tiny state-holder over `GenServer`                                   | [`agent.py`](src/fastactor/otp/agent.py)                                                        | [README §Agent](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#agent)                                            |
| `Task`, `TaskSupervisor`      | One-shot awaitable coroutines, linked or unlinked                    | [`task.py`](src/fastactor/otp/task.py)                                                          | [README §Task & TaskSupervisor](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#task--tasksupervisor)            |
| `handle_continue`             | Post-callback continuations                                          | [`process.py`](src/fastactor/otp/process.py), [`gen_server.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_server.py) | [README §handle_continue](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#handle_continue)                       |
| Named registration            | `name=` (global) vs `via=(registry, key)` at `start()`               | [`runtime.py`](src/fastactor/otp/runtime.py), [`registry.py`](src/fastactor/otp/registry.py)    | [README §Named registration](src/fastactor/otp/README.md#named-registration-at-start)        |

### Advanced primitives

| Primitive          | What it does                                                              | Source                                                                       |
| ------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `GenEvent`         | Event manager hosting many `EventHandler`s with their own state           | [`gen_event.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_event.py)                             |
| `GenStage`         | Demand-driven backpressured pipelines (`Producer`/`Consumer`/`ProducerConsumer` + 3 dispatchers) | [`gen_stage.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_stage.py)         |
| `GenStateMachine`  | OTP `gen_statem` FSMs with `Reply`/`NextEvent`/`Postpone`/state & generic timeouts | [`gen_state_machine.py`](src/fastactor/otp/gen_state_machine.py)    |

### Cross-cutting

| Concern                    | What it does                                                                           | Source                                                                                          |
| -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Messages / wire structs    | `Pid`, `Call`, `CallReply`, `Cast`, `Info`, `Stop`, `Exit`, `Down`, `Subscribe`, `SubscribeAck`, `Cancel`, `Demand`, `Events`, `Continue`, `Ignore`, `CrashReason` | [`_messages.py`](src/fastactor/otp/_messages.py)               |
| Exceptions                 | `Crashed`, `Failed`, `Shutdown`, `AlreadyRegistered`                                   | [`_exceptions.py`](src/fastactor/otp/_exceptions.py)                                            |
| Telemetry (optional)       | OpenTelemetry traces and metrics; gated by `[otel]` extra                              | [`telemetry.py`](src/fastactor/telemetry.py)                                                    |

---

## The Runtime

**Source:** [`src/fastactor/otp/runtime.py`](src/fastactor/otp/runtime.py)

Everything runs inside an active `Runtime`. It owns the anyio task group, the top-level `RuntimeSupervisor` (with `trap_exits=True`), the process registries (`processes` by id, `registry` for named lookup, `registries` for `Registry` entries), and a bounded crash log on the root supervisor.

```python
from fastactor.otp import Runtime

async def main():
    async with Runtime() as rt:
        # all actor work happens here
        ...
```

Inside any actor (or coroutine running inside the runtime), reach it with `Runtime.current()`. Only one Runtime may be active at a time — a second `async with Runtime():` raises `RuntimeError("Runtime already started")`.

### Entry points

Three equivalent ways to bring up a Runtime. All install `SIGINT` / `SIGTERM` traps by default (opt out via `trap_signals=False`); receiving a signal cancels the runtime's task group and triggers `__aexit__` cleanup inside the live loop.

```python
# 1. Context manager — primary. Best for tests, library code, anywhere inside an existing async scope.
async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        counter = await Counter.start()
        ...
anyio.run(main)

# 2. fastactor.run(main) — one-line app entry point. Wraps anyio.run + Runtime + clean-Ctrl-C handling.
import fastactor
fastactor.run(main)                     # returns main's return value
fastactor.run(main, trap_signals=False) # opt out of signal handling

# 3. Runtime.start() / Runtime.stop() — explicit pair. Best for REPLs/Jupyter where `async with` is awkward.
rt = await Runtime.start()              # enters the runtime; returns it
counter = await Counter.start()
await rt.stop()                         # equivalent to leaving `async with`
```

`Runtime.start()` enforces the singleton the same way the context manager does. `Runtime.stop()` is idempotent after the first call clears the singleton. Python's `atexit` runs synchronously after the loop is closed, so signal traps are the only reliable way to cleanly tear down on Ctrl-C — that's why `trap_signals=True` is the default.

### `whereis` — global lookup

```python
from fastactor.otp import whereis

proc = await whereis("my_singleton")
proc = await whereis(("sessions", user_id))
```

Accepts either a string (global name, registered via `name=` at start) or a `(registry, key)` tuple.

---

## Messages & wire safety

**Source:** [`_messages.py`](src/fastactor/otp/_messages.py)

The mailbox transports tagged `msgspec.Struct` envelopes. The base `Message` carries `sender_id: Pid | None` and an optional `metadata: dict[str, Any]`. Concrete variants:

- **Generic OTP control:** `Info`, `Stop`, `Exit`, `Down`, `Continue`, `Ignore`.
- **Synchronous request/reply:** `Call[Req, Res]` (carries a `ref: str`); reply envelope `CallReply` (`ref`, `result`).
- **Fire-and-forget:** `Cast[Req]`.
- **GenStage subscription protocol:** `Subscribe`, `SubscribeAck`, `Demand`, `Events`, `Cancel`.
- **Wire-safe crash payload:** `CrashReason` (frozen `msgspec.Struct` with `class_name`, `repr`, `traceback_str` — uses `tblib` if installed).

### `Pid` is the stable identity

```python
class Pid(msgspec.Struct, frozen=True):
    id: str
    node: str = ""        # "" → local; non-empty → remote (future)
```

`Pid` is what gets stamped onto every framework message. `message.sender_id` is the durable handle. `message.sender` is a property that resolves it back to a live local `Process` via `Runtime.current().processes`, falling back to a strong `_sender_ref` stashed at send time. The fallback means **a message can outlive its sender** — `message.sender` may still return the original `Process` even after it has terminated and been unregistered.

### The deferred-reply mechanism

The user-facing API is unchanged: stash the `Call` from `handle_call`, return `None`, then later call `saved_call.set_result(v)`. Internally, `set_result` calls `caller._deliver_reply(ref, value)` to populate the caller's pending-reply side-table, which `await server.call(...)` is waiting on. `CallReply` is exported as a wire envelope but the local in-process reply path does not currently emit it.

### Telemetry metadata

Every framework message has an optional `metadata: dict[str, Any]`. The OTel integration uses this to carry trace context across mailbox boundaries; you can also stamp your own keys.

---

## Idiomatic patterns

### 1. Runtime per program, not per feature

```python
async def main():
    async with Runtime():
        # build your supervision tree here
        ...

if __name__ == "__main__":
    anyio.run(main)
```

Never open two Runtimes concurrently. For tests, use the autouse `runtime` fixture in [`src/tests/conftest.py`](src/tests/conftest.py).

### 2. Build a supervision tree top-down

```python
async with Runtime() as rt:
    app_sup = await Supervisor.start_link()
    db = await app_sup.start_child(app_sup.child_spec("db", DbConn, restart="permanent"))
    pool = await app_sup.start_child(app_sup.child_spec(
        "pool",
        DynamicSupervisor,
        kwargs={"max_children": 50},
        restart="permanent",
        type="supervisor",
    ))
```

Hang your supervisor under the runtime root, then hang workers under your supervisor — not directly under the runtime root.

### 3. Deterministic cast sync via call round-trip

```python
server.cast(("set", key, value))
await server.call("sync")           # round-trip guarantees the cast has been processed
```

The mailbox is strictly FIFO. A successful `call` reply proves every prior `cast` has already been handled. Make sure the server returns a non-None reply for your sync key — the default `handle_call` returns `None`, which is NoReply and will hang. **Use this in tests instead of `asyncio.sleep`.**

### 4. `start_link` by default, `start` for detached work

`start_link` cascades failures through the supervision tree — that's the point of OTP. `start` is for work where a crash must not propagate (audit logging, background cleanup, best-effort emit).

### 5. Trap exits to build a monitor

```python
class Watchdog(GenServer):
    trap_exits = True
    async def handle_exit(self, msg: Exit):
        logger.error("child %s died: %s", msg.sender, msg.reason)
        # decide: restart? page? escalate?
```

### 6. Task for bounded parallelism

```python
tasks = [await Task.start(fetch, url) for url in urls]
results = [await t for t in tasks]
```

For unbounded parallelism with backpressure, use a `TaskSupervisor` with `max_children`.

### 7. Agent for "just hold this value"

Don't write a full GenServer for a counter. Use `Agent`. If your state manipulation is complex enough to need match/case, promote to GenServer.

### 8. Pick the right primitive by intent

- One process, sync request/reply or fire-and-forget → **`GenServer`**.
- One process, many independent observers each with their own state → **`GenEvent`**.
- A streaming pipeline that must respect downstream backpressure → **`GenStage`**.
- A finite-state protocol with state-specific event handling and timers → **`GenStateMachine`**.
- "Just hold this value" → **`Agent`**.
- One-shot awaitable coroutine → **`Task`**.

---

## Common gotchas

### `handle_call` / `handle_cast` receive wrappers, not raw requests

The callback signature is `handle_call(self, call: Call)`, not `handle_call(self, request, ...)`. Unpack with `call.message`:

```python
# correct
async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
    match call.message:
        case ("get", key): return self.store[key]

# WRONG
async def handle_call(self, request, from_, state): ...
```

### `cast` is synchronous (not awaitable)

`cast` calls `send_nowait` under the hood. It does *not* return a coroutine. Don't `await` it.

```python
server.cast("msg")          # correct
await server.cast("msg")    # TypeError — cast returns None
```

### Only one Runtime at a time

Nesting `async with Runtime():` raises. If you need child runtimes (tests, etc.), make sure each enters and exits cleanly.

### `handle_call` returning `None` means NoReply, not "reply with None"

A `handle_call` body that falls through to `None` (including the default base-class implementation) does NOT resolve the caller's `await server.call(...)` — the caller stays blocked until something calls `call.set_result(value)`. To reply with the *literal* value `None`, either use a tuple form (`return (None, Continue("ok"))`) or resolve the Call explicitly. In practice, pick a meaningful non-None reply (`True`, `"ok"`, the new state). The user-facing deferred-reply API is unchanged even though the implementation is now `ref` + caller side-table, not a per-`Call` event.

### `call` timeout raises, doesn't return None

```python
try:
    result = await server.call(req, timeout=1)
except TimeoutError:
    ...
```

### Exceptions in `handle_call` propagate in BOTH directions

The caller sees the exception (good), and the server crashes (also good — this is Elixir semantics). If you want to reply with an error value instead, construct it explicitly:

```python
async def handle_call(self, call: Call):
    try:
        return self._work(call.message)
    except KnownError as e:
        return {"error": str(e)}        # caller gets the dict, server does NOT crash
```

### `current_process` chooses the default outbound sender; `message.sender` is resolved

Inside a callback, the `current_process` ContextVar is set to the handling process, so `server.call(req)` / `server.cast(req)` default `sender` to `self` when you omit it. But received envelopes store `sender_id: Pid | None`, not a raw `Process`. `message.sender` is a property that resolves `sender_id` through `Runtime.current().processes` or a send-time `_sender_ref`. If you need durable identity, or you see `message.sender is None`, inspect `message.sender_id`. (`Process.info()` still defaults to the supervisor unless you pass `sender=` explicitly.)

### `Pid` is identity; `Process` is the live object

Use `Pid` / `message.sender_id` for durable identity and equality across messages. `Process.__eq__` only compares with other `Process` instances by `id`; a `Pid` and a `Process` are different types and do not compare equal.

### `message.sender` may be `None` even when `sender_id` is present

Returns `None` for remote pids (`sender_id.node != ""`), unresolved local ids (process already torn down with no `_sender_ref`), or messages decoded outside an active `Runtime`.

### `CallReply` exists, but the local `call()` path does not currently emit it

Treat `CallReply` as an exported wire envelope type, not as the mechanism `await server.call()` listens for. Local replies go through the caller-side pending-reply table.

### `CrashReason` is data, not an exception

`CrashReason` is a frozen `msgspec.Struct` — use it as a wire/logging payload, not as something you `raise` or `except`. Live `Exit` / `Down` propagation still carries raw `reason: Any`.

### `Any` payloads are local-friendly, not automatically wire-safe

`Call.message`, `Cast.message`, `Info.message`, and reason fields are typed `Any`. In-process actors can pass arbitrary Python objects, but only structured / serializable payloads are safe for msgspec round-trips. Avoid lambdas, coroutine objects, live `Process` references, and other runtime-only objects when wire-safety matters.

### Normal exit reasons are a whitelist

Only `"normal"`, `"shutdown"`, and `Shutdown(...)` are normal. Anything else — including `None`, `""`, `"ok"` — is treated as abnormal and cascades across links / triggers `transient` restarts. `is_normal_shutdown_reason` in `_exceptions.py` is the single source of truth.

### `brutal_kill` skips `terminate`

A child with `shutdown="brutal_kill"` has its mailbox closed immediately. `terminate()` won't run to completion, which means monitors may still see `Down` but cleanup you wrote in `terminate` won't fire. Use integer timeouts by default.

### `DynamicSupervisor` only supports `one_for_one`

Passing any other strategy raises `ValueError` in `init`. The error surfaces as the original exception from `start()` — don't waste time trying `"one_for_all"` there.

### `register_name` vs `Registry.register`

Different things:

- `Runtime.register_name` / `Process.start(name=...)` — **one** global name per process. Collides across the whole runtime. Backed by `runtime.registry` / `runtime._reverse_registry`.
- `Registry.register(name, key, proc)` — scoped to a named registry, can have many keys per process, and supports `unique` or `duplicate` modes. Backed by `runtime.registries`.

Use `name=` for singletons. Use `Registry` for everything else.

### `Task.start()` is unlinked — the caller survives a crash

If your task crashes and you don't `await` it, the exception is silently lost. Always `await` a task you care about, or use `Task.start_link(...)` so the cascade kills the caller too.

---

## Repo workflow & settings

Python is pinned via `mise.toml` (3.13.7); deps are managed by [uv](https://docs.astral.sh/uv/). Day-to-day work goes through `just` recipes ([`justfile`](justfile)):

```bash
uv sync                 # install / sync dependencies
just test               # full non-e2e suite (skips src/tests/e2e/)
just test gen_server    # filter by name (-k)
just test e2e           # run only the e2e suite
just debug              # stop at first failure, drop into ipdb
just lint               # ruff check + ty check
just fmt                # ruff format + ruff check --fix
just repl               # ipython with `from fastactor.otp import *`
```

Single test:

```bash
uv run pytest src/tests/otp/test_gen_server.py::test_call_returns_echo_payload
```

Optional OpenTelemetry integration: `pip install fastactor[otel]`, then either pass `Runtime(telemetry=True)` or set `FASTACTOR_TELEMETRY_ENABLED=1`. See [`src/fastactor/telemetry.py`](src/fastactor/telemetry.py).

There is no PyPI release yet — install from source:

```bash
uv add "fastactor @ git+https://github.com/CyrusNuevoDia/fastactor"
```

Runtime defaults live in `fastactor.settings.Settings` (pydantic-settings). Override with `FASTACTOR_`-prefixed env vars.

| Setting                   | Env var                             | Default      |
| ------------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ------------ |
| `mailbox_size`            | `FASTACTOR_MAILBOX_SIZE`            | `1024`       |
| `call_timeout`            | `FASTACTOR_CALL_TIMEOUT`            | `5`          |
| `stop_timeout`            | `FASTACTOR_STOP_TIMEOUT`            | `60`         |
| `supervisor_max_restarts` | `FASTACTOR_SUPERVISOR_MAX_RESTARTS` | `1`          |
| `supervisor_max_seconds`  | `FASTACTOR_SUPERVISOR_MAX_SECONDS`  | `5.0`        |
| `telemetry_enabled`       | `FASTACTOR_TELEMETRY_ENABLED`       | `False`      |
| `telemetry_service_name`  | `FASTACTOR_TELEMETRY_SERVICE_NAME`  | `"fastactor"`|

The mailbox size caps each process's inbox. `send_nowait` will raise `anyio.WouldBlock` if the mailbox is full.

---

## Testing & behavior

The test tree is split:

- [`src/tests/otp/`](src/tests/otp) — unit tests for every OTP module, plus [`SPEC.md`](src/tests/otp/SPEC.md), the source-of-truth behavior spec used to drive development.
- [`src/tests/tutorial/`](src/tests/tutorial) — 12-part actor-model walkthrough; each file is also the lesson, with a [`README.md`](src/tests/tutorial/README.md) for narrative order.
- [`src/tests/e2e/`](src/tests/e2e) — multi-agent system demos (chaos monkey, adaptive pool, consensus voting, hierarchical planning, racing speculators, compute/state split). Skipped by `just test`; run explicitly via `just test e2e`.
- [`src/tests/test_telemetry.py`](src/tests/test_telemetry.py) — covers the optional OTEL integration.

Shared `GenServer` doubles — `EchoServer`, `CounterServer`, `BoomServer`, `SlowStopServer`, `MonitorServer`, `LinkServer`, `ContinueServer`, `OrderLog`, `await_child_restart` — live in [`src/tests/otp/helpers.py`](src/tests/otp/helpers.py). Reuse them rather than rolling minimal subclasses in each test file. E2E helpers live separately in [`src/tests/e2e/helpers.py`](src/tests/e2e/helpers.py).

For deterministic-sync patterns (call round-trip, `await proc.stopped()`, `MonitorServer.await_events(n)`), see [`src/tests/README.md`](src/tests/README.md).

---

## Public surface & source layout

The authoritative export list is `fastactor.otp.__all__` in [`src/fastactor/otp/__init__.py`](src/fastactor/otp/__init__.py); top-level `fastactor` exports `run`, `settings`, `telemetry`. Read source in this order to absorb the design: `_messages.py` → `_exceptions.py` → `process.py` → `gen_server.py` → `supervisor.py` → `runtime.py`. The rest (`agent.py`, `dynamic_supervisor.py`, `registry.py`, `task.py`, `gen_event.py`, `gen_stage.py`, `gen_state_machine.py`, `telemetry.py`) layer onto that core.

```
src/fastactor/
├── __init__.py                         # run, settings, telemetry
├── settings.py                         # pydantic-settings
├── telemetry.py                        # OpenTelemetry integration (optional [otel] extra)
├── utils.py                            # id_generator (ksuids), emit_awaited
└── otp/
    ├── __init__.py                     # public re-exports
    ├── README.md                       # per-primitive API reference (core primitives)
    ├── _exceptions.py                  # Crashed, Failed, Shutdown, AlreadyRegistered, is_normal_shutdown_reason
    ├── _messages.py                    # Pid, Message, Call/CallReply/Cast/Info/Stop/Exit/Down, GenStage envelopes, Continue/Ignore, CrashReason
    ├── process.py                      # Process (base actor, lifecycle, links/monitors)
    ├── gen_server.py                   # GenServer
    ├── gen_event.py                    # GenEvent, EventHandler, RemoveHandler, SwapHandler, HandlerNotInstalled
    ├── gen_stage.py                    # GenStage, Producer, Consumer, ProducerConsumer, dispatchers
    ├── gen_state_machine.py            # GenStateMachine, Reply, NextEvent, Postpone, StateTimeout, Timeout, Hibernate
    ├── supervisor.py                   # Supervisor, ChildSpec, strategies, restart intensity
    ├── dynamic_supervisor.py           # DynamicSupervisor
    ├── registry.py                     # Registry, AlreadyRegistered
    ├── agent.py                        # Agent
    ├── task.py                         # Task, TaskSupervisor
    └── runtime.py                      # Runtime, RuntimeSupervisor, whereis, current_process ContextVar

src/tests/
├── README.md                           # testing patterns, fixtures, doubles
├── conftest.py                         # anyio_backend, runtime, supervisor, make_supervisor
├── test_telemetry.py                   # OTEL integration coverage
├── otp/                                # unit tests + SPEC.md (authoritative behavior)
│   ├── SPEC.md
│   ├── helpers.py                      # shared GenServer doubles
│   └── test_*.py
├── tutorial/                           # 12-part walkthrough (also the lesson)
│   ├── README.md
│   └── test_*.py
└── e2e/                                # multi-agent system demos (skipped by `just test`)
    ├── helpers.py
    └── test_*.py
```

---

## Prior art & further reading

Erlang/OTP and Elixir's `GenServer` / `GenEvent` / `GenStage` / `gen_statem` / `Supervisor` / `DynamicSupervisor` / `Registry` / `Agent` / `Task` modules are the direct inspiration.

---

MIT — see `LICENSE`.
